dataset: Haiti Historical Patterns — Current Crisis Extension
generated_by: AYITI INTEL Dashboard — Current Crisis Research (ayitiintel.com)
description: Additive extension to the core Haiti Historical Patterns dataset. Adds 3 new themes covering the 2015–2026 current crisis window: electoral collapse sequences, post-2021 gang consolidation, and the MSS/GSF international security intervention. Extends the core 46-event database with 42 new events (total: 88 events across 6 themes + 7 sub-periods).
rationale: The core dataset (v1.0.0) documents structural dependency cycles from 1825–2004. This extension provides the current-window patterns needed for daily intelligence briefs: gang territorial control benchmarks, electoral failure sequences for political development analysis, government legitimacy tracking, and MSS/GSF deployment status for intervention analysis.

electoral_collapse.name: Electoral Collapse Sequences (2015–2026)
electoral_collapse.summary: Three consecutive electoral failures — the annulled 2015 presidential election, the low-legitimacy 2016 redo (Moïse, <10% turnout), and the 2021–26 total suspension — constitute a structural pattern: each crisis is instrumentalized by external and domestic actors to consolidate executive power and defer accountability.

gang_consolidation_2021.name: Gang Consolidation & State Collapse (2021–present)
gang_consolidation_2021.summary: The July 2021 Moïse assassination created a power vacuum immediately exploited by gang federations. G9 (est. June 2020) and G-Pèp escalated rival control over Port-au-Prince. The Feb 2024 Viv Ansanm merger of both coalitions — coordinated with Henry's Kenya trip — drove 85% gang control of the capital, 1M+ IDPs, and the forced resignation of PM Ariel Henry, triggering a transitional presidential council (CPT) whose mandate expired Feb 7 2026 without elections.

mss_gsf.name: MSS/GSF International Security Intervention (2023–present)
mss_gsf.summary: In October 2023, the UN Security Council authorized a Kenya-led Multinational Security Support (MSS) mission after Haiti's 2021 request. Chronic underfunding ($600M annual cost, voluntary contributions only), judicial delays in Kenya, and Viv Ansanm resistance limited deployment to ~1,000 of 2,500 authorized personnel by late 2024. In October 2025, the UN transitioned the MSS into a 'Gang Suppression Force' (GSF) with a harder mandate. First GSF troops (Chad) arrived April 1, 2026; full operational capacity expected October 2026.

new_field.notes: Extended analytical notes field (present in extension events only). Provides sourced context, key figures, and brief references for each event. Not in v1.0.0 events.

events.event: Parliament dissolved — Martelly rules by decree
events.notes: Terms of 10 senators and 99 deputies expire; no elections held since 2012. Martelly dissolves parliament and begins ruling by decree, appointing unconstitutional electoral council.

events.event: First round presidential election — fraud allegations
events.notes: Haitian Bald Head (Tet Kale) candidate Moïse declared first-round winner. Mass protests erupt; multiple candidates allege systematic fraud. Runoff twice postponed then cancelled.

events.event: Presidential runoff cancelled — 2nd electoral failure
events.notes: Runoff cancelled 2 days before scheduled vote due to mass protests. Provisional president Jocelerme Privert installed. Opens 10-month electoral vacuum.

events.event: Martelly term ends — no successor; constitutional vacuum
events.notes: First explicit constitutional vacuum since Duvalier era. Privert serves as provisional president without electoral mandate for 10 months.

events.event: Moïse elected with <10% of eligible voters
events.notes: Third attempt at presidential election. Moïse wins with approximately 55% of votes cast but turnout is ~21% — <10% of eligible voters. Legitimacy fundamentally compromised from day one.

events.event: Moïse refuses to leave — term dispute triggers crisis
events.notes: Constitutional dispute: opposition argues Moïse's term ended Feb 7 2021; Moïse argues it ends Feb 7 2022 (counting from 2016 election not 2017 inauguration). US CORE Group supports Moïse. Arrest of 23 people in alleged coup plot. Moïse rules by decree, dissolves most constitutional courts.

events.event: Moïse assassinated — 28 foreign mercenaries, no elections since
events.actor: Colombian mercenaries / CTU Security / Haitian-American plotters
events.notes: President Jovenel Moïse shot dead at home at 1am by 26 Colombian ex-soldiers and 2 Haitian-Americans. Wife Martine wounded. Three claimants emerge for leadership. No elected president since. Investigation derailed by threats; Henry implicated via phone records.

events.event: Gang offensive forces PM Henry to resign from abroad
events.notes: Henry stranded in Kenya during gang offensive; barred from returning to Haiti. Resigns April 25 by letter signed in Los Angeles. First PM forced from power by gang blockade. Last elections: 2016.

events.event: CPT inaugurated — 9-member transitional council (no elections mandate)
events.notes: 9-member Transitional Presidential Council (CPT) sworn in; mandate expires Feb 7 2026. Tasked with appointing PM, cabinet, electoral commission. Immediate internal conflicts and corruption allegations.

events.event: CPT ousts PM Conille — replaces with Fils-Aimé
events.actor: CPT / Garry Conille / Alix Didier Fils-Aimé
events.notes: PM Garry Conille removed after 5 months over cabinet reshuffle dispute. Replaced by businessman Alix Didier Fils-Aimé. Second PM turnover in <8 months; deepens institutional instability.

events.event: Electoral dates set: Aug 30 & Dec 6 2026 — feasibility questioned
events.notes: Transitional government announces first-round presidential election for Aug 30, 2026; second round Dec 6. Observers widely doubt elections can occur while gangs control 90% of capital and 1.4M are displaced.

events.event: CPT mandate expires — political limbo; Fils-Aimé sole executive
events.notes: CPT mandate expires without elections. US imposes visa sanctions on 4 CPT members to prevent dismissal of Fils-Aimé; dispatches warships to Bay of Port-au-Prince. Fils-Aimé becomes sole executive; no constitutional framework for succession. Last elected government: 2016.

events.event: G9 alliance founded by ex-cop Jimmy Chérizier (Barbecue)
events.notes: Former HNP officer Chérizier forms G9 (9→12 armed groups). Receives weapons, uniforms, and funding from Moïse government. Begins attacking anti-government gang strongholds. OFAC sanctions imposed Dec 2020 for 2018 La Saline massacre (71 killed).

events.event: Moïse assassination removes state gang patron — power vacuum
events.notes: Moïse had been primary state patron of G9. Assassination removes central arbiter; rival G-Pèp forms in response to G9. Gang violence between factions increases 79% 2021–2022; 500+ fatalities in 2022.

events.event: Cité Soleil massacre — G9 kills 89+ civilians in territorial battle
events.notes: G9 assault on Cité Soleil (G-Pèp stronghold) kills 89–200+ civilians over 3 days. UN calls it deliberate targeting of civilians. Demonstrates strategic territorial consolidation by armed federations, not random violence.

events.event: Haiti formally requests international security force
events.notes: PM Ariel Henry formally requests multinational security mission. Triggers 12-month diplomatic process before UNSC authorizes in Oct 2023. Signals complete failure of HNP to contain gang expansion.

events.event: Viv Ansanm truce announced — G9 and G-Pèp merge
events.notes: Rival gang federations G9 and G-Pèp announce Viv Ansanm ('Live Together') alliance. Initially seen as fragile; gang clashes continue but sharply decline. Strategic realignment from inter-gang war to unified governance/expansion model.

events.event: Viv Ansanm coordinated assault — airports, prisons, police HQ
events.notes: Synchronized assault across Port-au-Prince: Toussaint Louverture airport closed, 2 major prisons stormed (4,000 inmates escape), multiple police stations burned, key roads blocked. Coincides with PM Henry's Kenya trip. ~80% of capital under gang control.

events.event: National Penitentiary stormed — 4,000 inmates escape
events.notes: Haiti's largest prison overrun; 4,000 inmates released including gang members, political detainees, and Moïse assassination suspects. Catastrophic blow to justice system. Airport closed since Feb 29 — country effectively blockaded.

events.event: Artibonite expansion — Pont-Sondé, Cabaret, Gressier seized
events.notes: Post-merger, Viv Ansanm expands beyond capital into Haiti's breadbasket Artibonite department and surrounding towns. Blocks National Route 1. Threatens food supply and provincial stability. 50,000+ flee Port-au-Prince.

events.event: Viv Ansanm massacre — 180+ killed in Port-au-Prince
events.notes: Viv Ansanm kills 180+ people in December 2024 — single-month peak of coalition violence. UN reports 5,600+ killed in 2024 — highest annual total since systematic tracking began. 1M+ internally displaced (3x increase in one year).

events.event: 85% capital control — 1M+ IDPs; criminal parallel governance
events.notes: GI-TOC report: gangs control 85% of capital; 1M+ IDPs (3x increase vs early 2024); gangs operating as parallel authorities with extortion systems, territorial rules, and governance structures. 5M in acute hunger.

events.event: Barbecue (Chérizier) indicted in US for sanctions violations
events.notes: US unseals indictment against Chérizier for conspiracy to violate OFAC sanctions — raising funds via diaspora in US. Evidence: money transfers, salary payments to gang members, firearms purchases. First direct US criminal charge against Viv Ansanm leadership.

events.event: Gangs expand to sea/road routes; 26 groups, ~90% capital control
events.notes: UN March 2026 report: 26 gangs control capital and surrounding areas with 'alarming levels of violence'; expansion to key maritime and road routes; push into Centre department. UN records 4,000+ killed in H1 2025; 1.4M displaced. HNP 'summary executions' allegations.

events.event: Haiti formally requests international security force from UN
events.notes: First formal request for armed international support since MINUSTAH's 2017 withdrawal. Triggers 12-month negotiation at UNSC — US pushes non-UN model to avoid assessed funding obligations.

events.event: Kenya agrees to lead multinational force — pledges 1,000 officers
events.notes: 10 months after Haiti's request, Kenya agrees after US pledges $300M and signs bilateral defense cooperation agreement. Sets precedent for African-led Caribbean security mission. Jamaica, Bahamas, Guyana, Benin, Bangladesh subsequently join.

events.event: UNSC Resolution 2699 — MSS authorized
events.notes: UNSC unanimously authorizes Multinational Security Support mission for 12 months (voluntary contributions only — no UN assessed funding). Mandated to support HNP counter-gang operations and protect critical infrastructure. Authorized ceiling: 2,500 personnel.

events.event: Kenyan High Court blocks deployment — constitutional challenge
events.notes: Kenyan court rules deployment unconstitutional in response to private citizen lawsuit. Deployment delayed months. Resolved by bilateral agreement signed between Kenya and Haiti on April 1, 2024. Illustrates fragility of voluntary mission model.

events.event: First MSS contingent lands — 400 Kenyan officers
events.notes: First 400 Kenyan elite police land — 4 months after Viv Ansanm offensive. Airport had been closed for months; just reopened. Second contingent of 200 arrives July 16. Caribbean contingent (Jamaica, Belize) arrives Sep 12. Mission severely understaffed vs 2,500 authorized ceiling.

events.event: MSS deployed ~600 officers; limited progress; funding crisis
events.notes: MSS has ~600 deployed (vs 2,500 authorized). Limited successes: partial airport retaking, joint HNP patrols. Ruto criticizes 'lack of equipment, logistics, and funds.' No tangible improvement in gang control. Blinken visits Sep 5, acknowledges funding gaps.

events.event: UNSC Resolution 2751 — MSS mandate renewed 1 year
events.notes: Unanimous renewal through Oct 2, 2025. Encourages accelerated deployment and additional voluntary contributions. Reflects international community's commitment despite effectiveness concerns. Debate continues on shifting to UN-assessed-budget peacekeeping model.

events.event: 3rd Kenyan contingent (217 officers) + 70 Salvadorans; US freezes funding
events.notes: Third Kenyan deployment brings total to ~1,000. Same day: US freezes MSS funding under new administration policy — acute financial crisis for mission. Raises doubts about long-term viability under voluntary-contributions model.

events.event: UN SG report rules out peacekeeping mission — conditions too dangerous
events.notes: Antnio Guterres report to UNSC: conditions on ground too dangerous for standard UN peacekeeping mission. Proposes instead a UN support office (UNSOH) for logistics. Effectively forecloses affordable UN-assessed-budget solution.

events.event: UNSC Resolution 2793 — MSS transitions to Gang Suppression Force (GSF)
events.notes: Unanimous resolution transitions MSS to 'Gang Suppression Force' — harder mandate, clearer targeting authority. Non-UN mission model retained. Kenyan force remains during transition; some contingents begin repatriation. GSF Special Representative appointed. UNSOH established for logistics.

events.event: UNSOH operational — Daniela Kroslak appointed head
events.notes: UN Support Office in Haiti (UNSOH) activated, begins providing logistical support to GSF. Resolution 2793 required UNSOH to assume full logistical responsibility within 6 months of Oct 2025 adoption.

events.event: First GSF troops arrive — Chad contingent (750 pledged)
events.notes: First non-Kenyan GSF ground troops arrive. Chad pledges 750 members; initial contingent lands Apr 1. Full deployment expected to bring GSF toward 5,500 target by summer 2026. Kenyan withdrawal timeline: ~October 2026. Full operational capacity: October 2026.

events.event: UNSC Resolution 2814 — BINUH renewed to Jan 2027
events.notes: Unanimous renewal of BINUH political mission through Jan 31, 2027. Requests SG report on DDR options. France, UK, Denmark, Latvia, Greece, Colombia, China, Russia all broke silence before adoption — reflects sustained multilateral engagement despite frustration.
